pattern: Swapping two values whose gcd exceeds one is really an edge between them in a graph, and any two values connected through a chain of such edges can end up in either order; so group every value with its prime factors under one union-find structure and check that each position's original value shares a group with the value that must end up there.
hints: Two numbers can be swapped directly only when they share a common factor greater than one, but repeated swaps let values move through anyone else reachable via a chain of shared factors.

Instead of connecting values pairwise (too slow for large arrays), connect each value to every prime factor it has, using a union-find whose universe spans both the numbers present and the small primes up to the largest value.

Sort a copy of the array; the original can be rearranged into that sorted order exactly when, at every position, the original value and the sorted value it must become belong to the same union-find group.

edge_inputs.description: all three values share pairwise factors
a value repeats but the group still cannot fully sort
several values chained through shared factors
single value is trivially already sorted
two coprime values out of order can never swap
two values sharing a factor swap into place
two equal values at the extreme upper bound
two separate connected groups that individually sort but not against each other

stress.description: large random arrays across the full value range
